is synergy holds the potential to revolutionize our understanding of microbial

communities and their intricate roles in various ecosystems. In the realm of metagenomics,

artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful ally, oﬀering an extensive toolbox of

techniques that are indispensable for unraveling the intricacies of microbial communities

(Dindhoria et al., 2024).is section delves into the core components of AI’s metagenomic

toolkit, with a particular focus on machine learning, deep learning, and natural language

processing (NLP). Machine learning stands as a foundational pillar of AI in metagenomics,

contributing signiﬁcantly to the classiﬁcation, prediction, and interpretation of the vast volume

of genetic data derived from microbial communities (Figure 3). Machine learning algorithms

are instrumental in taxonomic identiﬁcation and classiﬁcation, enabling the discrimination

of diverse microbial taxa based on their genetic proﬁles.Techniques such as Random Forest

and Support Vector Machines have proven to be invaluable in distinguishing microbiomes

from various environments, shedding light on the diversity and composition of microbial

communities. A noteworthy application of machine learning in metagenomics is the prediction

of gene functions. Machine learning models can forecast the likely functions of genes, even

when those functions remain elusive or uncharacterized (Molefe et al., 2021). By comparing

these unknown genes to known genes, functional annotation is improved, enriching our

understanding of the roles individual genes play within microbial ecosystems. Furthermore,

machine learning is a crucial tool in identifying microbial interactions within communities.

It assists in unraveling complex relationships, oﬀering insights into the dynamics of microbial

ecosystems. Machine learning’s utility in metagenomics extends to more personalized and

healthcare-oriented applications (Chukwuneme et al., 2021). For instance, machine learning

models can be employed to analyze the human gut microbiome composition and predict

health outcomes.is is particularly valuable in the context of personalized medicine, where

the microbiome’s role in various diseases, such as obesity and inﬂammatory bowel disease,

is of great interest (Arcila-Galvis et al., 2022; Chukwuneme et al., 2021). In the evolving

8

Genomic Intelligence: Metagenomics and Articial Intelligence

landscape of metagenomic research, the integration of multi-omics data is another exciting

area. Combining genomics, metagenomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics data with advanced

machine-learning techniques allows for a more comprehensive understanding of microbial

communities and their functions. Deep learning, a subset of machine learning, has emerged

as a transformative force in metagenomics. Leveraging neural networks, deep learning

techniques excel in uncovering the intricacies and complexity of microbial communities.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) have

proven particularly valuable in the analysis of metagenomic data, oﬀering capabilities such as

predicting gene functions, annotating genetic sequences, and uncovering intricate relationships

within metagenomic datasets. A remarkable facet of deep learning is its ability to predict

gene functions, even for genes with unknown or uncharacterized roles. is is achieved by

analyzing similarities between these unknown genes and their known counterparts, enriching

our understanding of the functional potential of microbial communities. Deep learning

also facilitates the exploration of microbial interactions within complex ecosystems. By

identifying and analyzing intricate relationships, researchers gain a deeper understanding of

how microorganisms cooperate or compete in various environmental niches. Deep learning’s

potential extends to the analysis of metagenomic data from diverse ecosystems,including soil,

marine environments, and the human microbiome. For example, in soil metagenomics, deep

learning has been employed to predict the functional properties of microbial communities

and their response to environmental changes, enhancing our understanding of soil health

and ecosystem dynamics. In the context of the human microbiome, deep learning models

are being utilized to analyze the complexities associated with various diseases, leading to

potential diagnostic and therapeutic advancements in precision medicine.

3\. Metagenomics Meets AI Applications

In a journey that takes us beyond the sequences, exploring the diverse applications of

metagenomics combined with artiﬁcial intelligence (AI).is voyage uncovers the potential

of AI in functional proﬁling,taxonomic composition analysis,discovering novel microbes and

genes, gaining insights into ecosystems, and harnessing metagenomics for biotechnological

advancements (Karatas et al., 2022). Metagenomics paired with AI empowers us to predict

the functional potential of microbial communities. It allows us to decipher the metabolic

pathways, enzyme functions, and biological processes encoded within the genes present in

these communities. By analyzing the genetic content, AI algorithms can provide valuable

insights into the capabilities of microbial ecosystems. One of the signiﬁcant applications

in this realm is the prediction of functional genes related to biogeochemical cycles, such

as nitrogen or carbon cycling. AI-driven functional proﬁling helps researchers understand

how microorganisms inﬂuence the biogeochemical processes of their environment, which

is essential for addressing ecological and environmental questions (Khanyile et al., 2023). In

recent years, AI-driven functional proﬁling has been instrumental in the discovery of novel

metabolic pathways in extreme environments. For example, AI analysis of metagenomic

data from hydrothermal vents unveiled the presence of unique genes responsible for the

oxidation of sulphur compounds, shedding light on the microbial processes underlying the

ecosystem’s energy ﬂow. Furthermore, metagenomics combined with AI has opened new

avenues in biotechnology. Functional proﬁling allows us to identify enzymes with potential

industrial applications, such as enzymes involved in lignocellulose degradation for biofuel

production. AI-driven functional predictions enable researchers to select the most promising

candidates for bioprospecting eﬀorts.Taxonomic composition analysis is a fundamental task

in metagenomics (Kumar et al., 2024). AI plays a crucial role in this aspect by providing

accurate and eﬃcient methods to identify and classify microorganisms. Machine learning

models, for instance, can assign taxonomic labels to sequences,aiding in the characterization

of the microbial community. AI-driven taxonomy untangling also supports the exploration

of uncharted ecosystems. Metagenomic data from diverse environments, such as extreme

environments or deep-sea habitats, can be eﬀectively analyzed using AI to reveal the taxonomic

diversity present, shedding light on the adaptability of microorganisms to various niches.

In recent research, AI-powered taxonomic analysis has been instrumental in uncovering

microbial diversity in extreme environments (Mbatha et al., 2023). For instance, metagenomic

data from deep-sea hydrothermal vents revealed the presence of unique archaeal lineages,

previously unknown to science. AI’s ability to classify these novel organisms has expanded

our understanding of life’s diversity in extreme conditions (Figure 2). Moreover, AI-driven

taxonomic analysis aids in assessing the impact of environmental changes on microbial

communities. For example, researchers have used AI to study the eﬀects of climate change

on Arctic microbiomes.

Fig 1.2 Biotechnological Applications Enabled by AI in Metagenomics

ese studies help in monitoring the resilience of ecosystems to anthropogenic

stressors and provide critical insights into environmental conservation eﬀorts (Pavlopoulos

et al., 2018; Ramadass et al., 2023). Metagenomics, coupled with AI, is a treasure trove for

discovering novel microbes and genes. AI algorithms can identify unique genetic sequences

or previously unknown microorganisms within metagenomic datasets. ese discoveries

expand our understanding of microbial diversity and their genetic potential. One practical

application is in bioprospecting. Metagenomic data, when analyzed using AI, can lead to the

identiﬁcation of novel enzymes or metabolic pathways with biotechnological applications.

is has implications in various industries, including pharmaceuticals, agriculture, and

bioremediation (Singh et al., 2024). AI’s ability to unearth novel microbes has been particularly

valuable in extreme environments. For example, AI-driven metagenomic analysis of acid mine

drainage communities led to the discovery of novel acidophilic microorganisms capable of

thriving in highly acidic conditions. ese ﬁndings have implications for biotechnological

applications, such as the bioleaching of metals from ores. AI-assisted metagenomics enables

the identiﬁcation of genes involved in the synthesis of bioactive compounds. In marine

metagenomic studies, AI algorithms have been employed to discover genes responsible for

the production of antimicrobial peptides, enzymes, and secondary metabolites.ese ﬁndings

have potential applications in drug discovery and biotechnology. AI is revolutionizing our

understanding of ecosystems by providing insights into microbial interactions and ecological

Genomic Intelligence: Metagenomics and Articial Intelligence

11

dynamics. It enables the modeling of complex ecological networks and the prediction of how

microbial communities respond to environmental changes. AI’s role in ecology extends to

ecosystem management and conservation. For instance, AI-driven models have been used

to predict the eﬀects of land-use changes on soil microbial communities. is information

is crucial for sustainable land management and biodiversity conservation eﬀorts. AI-driven

ecological models provide insights into how microbial communities respond to disturbances,

such as pollution or climate change (Suster et al., 2024). In aquatic ecosystems, AI has

been used to study the impact of contaminants on microbial communities, aiding in the

development of strategies for pollution control and ecosystem restoration. AI’s contribution

to ecology also includes the study of symbiotic relationships. For example, in agricultural

ecosystems, AI-powered analysis has been used to understand the dynamics of plant-microbe

interactions. is knowledge is essential for optimizing crop production and sustainable

agriculture. e biotechnology sector reaps the beneﬁts of metagenomics combined with

AI. AI-driven metagenomics accelerates the discovery of novel bioactive compounds,

enzymes, and microorganisms with biotechnological potential.is has applications in drug

discovery, bioremediation, and the development of sustainable bioprocesses. By leveraging

metagenomics and AI, researchers can unlock the biotechnological bounty hidden within

microbial communities and harness it for various industrial and medical purposes. e

synergy between metagenomics and AI has advanced the ﬁeld of drug discovery. AI-driven

metagenomics allows for the rapid identiﬁcation of microbial strains capable of producing

bioactive compounds, including antibiotics and anticancer agents (Tello et al., 2019). is

streamlines the drug discovery process and holds promise for the development of novel

therapeutics. In bioremediation, AI’s role is pivotal in optimizing the selection of microbial

consortia for environmental cleanup. AI algorithms analyze metagenomic data to identify

microorganisms with the potential to degrade pollutants or remediate contaminated sites.

is has applications in mitigating the impact of environmental pollution and restoring

ecosystems. Furthermore, the biotech sector beneﬁts from AI-assisted metagenomics in the

development of sustainable bioprocesses (Zhang & Itan, 2019). AI-driven analysis aids in

the identiﬁcation of enzymes and pathways essential for bioconversion processes, such as

lignocellulose degradation for biofuel production. is contributes to the advancement of

renewable and sustainable biotechnological solutions.

4\. Challenges and Ethical Consideration

As we journey through the intersection of metagenomics and artiﬁcial intelligence (AI), we

must navigate a complex landscape of challenges and ethical considerations. Metagenomic

data, though a valuable resource, poses a signiﬁcant challenge when integrating AI into

research (Papoutsoglou et al., 2023). e sheer volume, complexity, and variability of

metagenomic data demand sophisticated data processing and quality control measures.One

of the primary issues is the quality of data generated through high-throughput sequencing

technologies. Metagenomic data can be aﬀected by various biases, including ampliﬁcation

biases, sequencing errors, and contamination. AI models trained on low-quality data can

12

Genomic Intelligence: Metagenomics and Articial Intelligence

lead to erroneous conclusions and misinterpretation of microbial community dynamics

(Ogbuke et al.,2022).Data normalization is another critical aspect.AI algorithms need robust

normalization techniques to account for variations in sequencing depth and coverage,ensuring

that all microbial taxa are accurately represented in the analysis. Inadequate normalization

can lead to skewed results and misrepresentations of microbial diversity (Renzi et al., 2024).

Data integration across multiple omics levels, such as metagenomics, metatranscriptomics,

and metaproteomics, presents a formidable challenge (Whitmore et al., 2023; Yakovleva

et al., 2023). e integration of diverse data types into a cohesive framework for analysis

demands advanced AI techniques to reveal the intricate relationships between genes, proteins,

and metabolites within microbial communities. Addressing the data quality dilemma in

metagenomics and AI is essential to avoid misinterpretations, misrepresentations, and

ﬂawed conclusions. It requires the development of AI-driven data preprocessing and quality

control pipelines tailored to the unique challenges posed by metagenomic data(Sapoval et

al., 2022). e integration of metagenomics and AI also raises signiﬁcant ethical concerns,

particularly regarding privacy. Metagenomic data often contains sensitive information about

individuals, communities, or ecosystems. As researchers collect and analyze this data, they

must exercise caution to protect privacy and ensure ethical practices. Human microbiome

research, for example, may involve the collection of samples from individuals (Rane, 2023).

is data can reveal detailed information about an individual’s health, lifestyle, and genetic

makeup. Ethical considerations include obtaining informed consent, anonymizing data, and

safeguarding sensitive information from potential misuse. Privacy concerns also extend to

environmental metagenomics, where data may be linked to speciﬁc ecosystems, locations, or

environmental conditions. In these cases, the dissemination of data may have ecological and

conservation implications. Researchers must balance the desire for transparency and data

sharing with the need to protect sensitive ecosystems. AI plays a crucial role in addressing

these ethical concerns by enabling privacy-preserving data analysis.Techniques like federated

learning allow data to remain on local servers, and AI models are trained without centralizing

sensitive information. is approach protects privacy while still enabling valuable insights

from metagenomic data. In the emerging ﬁeld of metagenomics and AI, ethical research

practices are essential. Researchers must act as scientiﬁc guardians, upholding the principles

of integrity, transparency, and accountability. One aspect of ethical research is the responsible

use of AI. Models should be transparent and interpretable, enabling researchers to understand

how AI arrives at its conclusions. is transparency not only ensures ethical practices

but also enhances the trustworthiness of research ﬁndings. Additionally, research ethics

extends to the dissemination of results. Open science practices, such as sharing data, code,

and methodologies, promote transparency and facilitate the validation of research ﬁndings

by the scientiﬁc community. is open approach also ensures that metagenomic research

beneﬁts society as a whole.

Furthermore, ethical considerations involve the equitable distribution of research

beneﬁts. Metagenomic studies conducted in partnership with indigenous communities, for

example, should include mechanisms to share the beneﬁts of research, including knowledge

transfer, capacity building, and economic opportunities. As we embark on this exciting

journey at the intersection of metagenomics and AI, it is vital to uphold ethical standards

to ensure that research is conducted with the utmost integrity, transparency, and respect for

privacy and societal values.

5\. Collaborative Possibilities by Bridging the Data-Analysis Gap

At the convergence of metagenomics and artiﬁcial intelligence (AI), a harmonious symphony

of collaborative possibilities emerges. Metagenomics and AI bring complementary strengths

to the table, oﬀering the potential for transformative collaborations (Dudek et al., 2024). Here,

we delve into several collaborative avenues and their implications for science, technology,

and society. Metagenomics generates vast datasets with intricate biological information,

while AI excels in data analysis, pattern recognition, and prediction. Collaboration between

these ﬁelds bridges the data-analysis gap, enabling researchers to extract meaningful insights

from complex biological data (Kumar et al., 2023). In the context of microbial ecology,

metagenomics reveals the composition and functions of microbial communities, but making

sense of this data can be overwhelming. AI algorithms, such as machine learning and deep

learning, are proﬁcient in identifying ecological patterns, uncovering microbial interactions,

and predicting ecosystem dynamics. Metagenomics and AI provide a comprehensive view

of microbial ecosystems,from taxonomic composition to functional roles.is collaborative

synergy extends to applications in precision medicine. Metagenomics helps uncover the

human microbiome’s impact on health, while AI analyses this information to predict disease

14

Genomic Intelligence: Metagenomics and Articial Intelligence

risk, identify biomarkers, and personalize treatment strategies. Such collaborations hold the

promise of revolutionizing healthcare by oﬀering tailored interventions for individuals based

on their unique microbial proﬁles.

5.1. Metagenomics Meets AI in Bioprospecting

e synergy between metagenomics and AI has the potential to accelerate drug discovery by

unlocking the vast biochemical diversity present in microbial communities. Metagenomics

allows us to access the genetic potential of uncultured microorganisms, while AI aids in

predicting and screening for bioactive compounds. In bioprospecting, AI-driven metagenomic

analysis enables the identiﬁcation of genes involved in the synthesis of novel enzymes, bioactive

secondary metabolites, and antibiotics (Kumar et al., 2023). ese ﬁndings open doors to

the development of new therapeutic agents and biotechnological solutions. AI algorithms

can predict the ecological roles of microorganisms and their potential to produce speciﬁc

bioactive compounds. Metagenomic data guides AI models to prioritize microbial strains

with desired bioprospecting traits, increasing the eﬃciency of screening processes.

5.2. AI-Informed Conservation through Metagenomics

Conservation eﬀorts in various ecosystems, from rainforests to coral reefs, beneﬁt from the

collaboration between metagenomics and AI. Metagenomic data reveals the diversity and

functions of microbial communities crucial to ecosystem health, while AI aids in ecosystem

monitoring and decision-making. AI-driven ecological models can predict how environmental

changes, such as deforestation or climate change, impact microbial communities and ecosystem

services. Such models enable informed conservation strategies that protect both biodiversity

and ecosystem functions (Sheng et al., 2023; Woodrich & Pape, 2024). For example, in

marine ecosystems, metagenomics and AI are used to study the impact of pollution on

coral reefs. Metagenomic data reveals the response of microbial communities to pollutants,

while AI models predict the ecological consequences (Zhang et al., 2023). is knowledge

informs targeted conservation eﬀorts to mitigate damage and support coral reef resilience.

5.3. Data Sharing and Collaborative Research Networks

e collaborative possibilities extend beyond research synergy to global data sharing and

collaborative research networks. Metagenomic datasets are invaluable resources that transcend

geographical boundaries. International collaborations facilitated by AI-driven data-sharing

platforms enhance the accessibility of these datasets to researchers worldwide. Eﬀorts

like the Earth Microbiome Project and the International Human Microbiome Standards

promote the sharing of metagenomic data, fostering collaborations among researchers and

institutions (Voth & Khanna, 2020; Zhao et al., 2022).ese initiatives promote standardized

data collection, integration, and analysis, ensuring data quality and comparability. AI-driven

data-sharing platforms play a pivotal role in ensuring data interoperability, making it easier

for researchers to collaborate, exchange ﬁndings, and build upon each other’s work. ese

collaborative networks hold the potential to accelerate scientiﬁc discoveries and address

Genomic Intelligence: Metagenomics and Articial Intelligence

15

global challenges, such as antimicrobial resistance and ecosystem conservation.

5.4. Ethics, Governance, and Interdisciplinary Collaboration

As metagenomics and AI continue to advance, interdisciplinary collaboration becomes

increasingly important. Ethical considerations, data governance, and societal impacts

require input from experts in diverse ﬁelds. Collaboration between microbiologists, data

scientists, ethicists, legal scholars, and policymakers ensures that research is conducted

with sensitivity to ethical concerns and societal values. is synergy extends to education

and outreach. Collaborations between scientists and educators facilitate the dissemination

of knowledge and promote scientiﬁc literacy. Public understanding of metagenomics, AI,

and their collaborative potential is essential to informed decision-making and responsible

technology use. e intersection of metagenomics and AI oﬀers a wealth of collaborative

possibilities that extend across scientiﬁc, technological, and ethical domains. By harnessing

their combined strengths, researchers can address complex challenges, advance knowledge,

and contribute to a better understanding of the world’s microbial and ecological intricacies.

6\. Conclusion and Future Trends

As we peer into the future of metagenomics and artiﬁcial intelligence (AI), the possibilities

seem boundless.is section explores the emerging trends and uncharted research frontiers

that will shape the evolution of this exciting intersection. e future of metagenomics and

AI holds the promise of groundbreaking discoveries and transformative applications. Several

emerging trends like artiﬁcial intelligence enhanced biotechnological innovations, long-read

sequencing and artiﬁcial intelligence, single-cell metagenomics, artiﬁcial intelligence -assisted

drug development, artiﬁcial intelligence in ecological forecasting etc., are set to deﬁne

this exciting journey. One emerging trend is the integration of single-cell metagenomics

with AI. is cutting-edge approach allows researchers to delve deeper into microbial

communities by analyzing individual cells. AI-driven analysis of single-cell data will

unveil new insights into microbial diversity, functions, and interactions. e combination

of long-read sequencing technologies and AI promises more accurate metagenomic data.

Long-read sequencing provides more complete genomic information, while AI algorithms

aid in error correction, genome assembly, and metagenome reconstruction. is trend will

enhance our understanding of complex microbial communities.e future will witness AI-

driven innovations in biotechnology. From the discovery of novel enzymes with industrial

applications to the optimization of bioprocesses, metagenomics, and AI will play a pivotal

role in advancing biotechnological solutions. is includes the development of sustainable

biofuels, bioplastics, and pharmaceuticals. e AI models will become indispensable for

ecological forecasting.ese models will predict how environmental changes, such as climate

change or land use, will impact microbial communities and ecosystems. is information

is crucial for conservation and ecosystem management. e future holds the potential for

AI-assisted drug development rooted in metagenomics. AI algorithms will predict bioactive

compounds and novel drug candidates, accelerating the drug discovery process. is trend

has far-reaching implications for precision medicine and the treatment of various diseases.

As we journey into the future, metagenomics and AI will unveil uncharted research frontiers

like ethical and legal frameworks global microbiome mapping, eco-metagenomics in extreme

environments, microbiome-brain connection, interdisciplinary collaborations that challenge

our understanding of the microbial world and its implications for science and society. e

link between the gut microbiome and brain health is a burgeoning frontier. Metagenomics

and AI will shed light on how the microbiome inﬂuences neurological and mental health.

Understanding this connection has potential applications in treating neurodegenerative

diseases and mental disorders. Metagenomics and AI will take us to the Earth’s most extreme

environments, from deep-sea hydrothermal vents to subglacial lakes. Research in these hostile

environments will uncover novel microbial life forms and metabolic processes. AI will assist

in deciphering the genetic and functional adaptations of extremophiles. e creation of a

comprehensive global microbiome map is on the horizon. Metagenomic data from diverse

ecosystems and human populations worldwide will be integrated and analyzed using AI.is

endeavor will provide a holistic understanding of microbial biodiversity, biogeography, and

their inﬂuence on health and the environment. As metagenomics and AI expand their reach,

ethical and legal considerations will become paramount. e development of international

ethical frameworks and legal regulations for data sharing, privacy protection, and equitable

beneﬁts from research will be a crucial frontier in ensuring responsible innovation.e future

will witness deeper collaborations across diverse disciplines. Microbiologists, data scientists,

ethicists, policymakers, and experts from various ﬁelds will work together to address the

multifaceted challenges and opportunities at the intersection of metagenomics and AI.

Interdisciplinary research will drive innovation and responsible technology use. As we gaze

into the crystal ball of metagenomics meeting AI, we anticipate an era of unprecedented

scientiﬁc discovery, technological innovation, and ethical exploration. e synergy of these

two ﬁelds promises to reshape our understanding of the microbial world and its profound

implications for human health, ecosystems, and biotechnology.

[AI-Driven Precision Fermentation: From Restaurant Food Waste to Sustainable Protein Production](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/394099616_AI-Driven_Precision_Fermentation_From_Restaurant_Food_Waste_to_Sustainable_Protein_Production)

Article

Full-text available

- Jul 2025
- FOOD BIOPROCESS TECH

- [![Nida Kanwal](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Nida Kanwal](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Nida-Kanwal-5)
- [![Min Zhang](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Min Zhang](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Min-Zhang-138)
- [![Salman Khan](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Salman Khan](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Salman-Khan-166)
- [![Uzma Batool](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Uzma Batool](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Uzma-Batool-8)

Restaurant food waste is used as feedstock. It is transformed into high-value protein through precision fermentation. This process is powered by AI and automation, revolutionizing sustainable protein production. This review explores AI-driven precision fermentation as a solution to the growing global protein demand. The demand is projected to increase by 70% by 2050. At the same time, this technology aims to reduce the 931 million tons of food waste generated globally each year. Precision fermentation leverages machine learning for strain optimization and digital twins for bioprocess simulation. Autonomous bioreactors enable real-time adaptive control. This technology converts nutrient-rich food waste into functional proteins. It produces up to 90% fewer greenhouse gas emissions compared to conventional animal sources. The paper explores the technical foundations of microbial engineering. It also examines the role of robotics in high-throughput fermentation screening. Additionally, the integration of IoT and blockchain enhances process transparency and traceability. It highlights case studies and pilot programs in the USA, France, Brazil, and China, identifying opportunities and bottlenecks in feedstock variability, regulations, and public acceptance. Key research gaps include the need for AI models that handle noisy biological data and standardized platforms for academic-industry collaboration. The findings emphasize the potential of decentralized biomanufacturing hubs to support local food systems, reduce urban waste, and boost environmental resilience. This review highlights AI-driven precision fermentation to convert food waste into high-value protein, addressing both waste reduction and protein sustainability. With AI as a catalytic layer, this approach offers a scalable, economically viable model for future food systems.

[AI in Drug Discovery: Accelerating Pharmaceutical Research](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/386143116_AI_in_Drug_Discovery_Accelerating_Pharmaceutical_Research)

Article

Full-text available

- Jan 2024

- [Muneeb Kiani](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Muneeb-Kiani-2298525530)
- [Fizan Nasir](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Fizan-Nasir-2298547563)
- [![Amelia Ethan](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Amelia Ethan](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Amelia-Ethan)

The field of pharmaceutical research is undergoing a transformative revolution, driven by the rapid integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques. This paper delves into the profound impact of AI on drug discovery, elucidating its role in accelerating the development of novel pharmaceuticals. It navigates through the conventional drug discovery process, outlining the challenges inherent in its linear and time-consuming nature. Subsequently, this paper unveils the fundamental aspects of AI, emphasizing its utility in deciphering the complex web of biological data. The relentless pursuit of novel therapeutic agents to combat diseases has been a cornerstone of medical advancement. However, the traditional drug discovery process has long been marred by time-consuming experimentation and astronomical costs. In response, the pharmaceutical industry has turned to Artificial Intelligence (AI) as a game-changing force in drug discovery. This paper delves deep into the realm of AI-driven drug discovery, shedding light on how machine learning, data analysis, and predictive modeling are revolutionizing the field. We explore AI's contribution to target identification and validation, as well as its pivotal role in reshaping high-throughput screening and hit-to-lead optimization. Additionally, AI's influence in lead optimization and preclinical testing is dissected, elucidating its ability to expedite critical phases of drug development. Ethical considerations surrounding AI-driven drug discovery are discussed, underscoring the importance of data quality, interpretability, and ethical safeguards. The paper concludes by projecting the future landscape of pharmaceutical research with AI as its driving force, envisaging a more efficient, cost-effective, and innovative era in drug discovery. [Networking the desert plant microbiome, bacterial and fungal symbionts structure and assortativity in co-occurrence networks](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/383686239_Networking_the_desert_plant_microbiome_bacterial_and_fungal_symbionts_structure_and_assortativity_in_co-occurrence_networks)

Article

Full-text available

- Sep 2024

- [![Kenji Maurice](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Kenji Maurice](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Kenji-Maurice)
- [![Liam Laurent-Webb](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Liam Laurent-Webb](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Liam-Laurent-Webb)
- [![Amélia Bourceret](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Amélia Bourceret](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Amelia-Bourceret)
- [![Marc Ducousso](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Marc Ducousso](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Marc-Ducousso)

In nature, microbes do not thrive in seclusion but are involved in complex interactions within- and between-microbial kingdoms. Among these, symbiotic associations with mycorrhizal fungi and nitrogen-fixing bacteria are namely known to improve plant health, while providing resources to benefit other microbial members. Yet, it is not clear how these microbial symbionts interact with each other or how they impact the microbiota network architecture. We used an extensive co-occurrence network analysis, including rhizosphere and roots samples from six plant species in a natural desert in AlUla region (Kingdom of Saudi Arabia) and described how these symbionts were structured within the plant microbiota network. We found that the plant species was a significant driver of its microbiota composition and also of the specificity of its interactions in networks at the microbial taxa level. Despite this specificity, a motif was conserved across all networks, i.e., mycorrhizal fungi highly covaried with other mycorrhizal fungi, especially in plant roots—this pattern is known as assortativity. This structural property might reflect their ecological niche preference or their ability to opportunistically colonize roots of plant species considered non symbiotic e.g., H. salicornicum, an Amaranthaceae. Furthermore, these results are consistent with previous findings regarding the architecture of the gut microbiome network, where a high level of assortativity at the level of bacterial and fungal orders was also identified, suggesting the existence of general rules of microbiome assembly. Otherwise, the bacterial symbionts Rhizobiales and Frankiales covaried with other bacterial and fungal members, and were highly structural to the intra- and inter-kingdom networks. Our extensive co-occurrence network analysis of plant microbiota and study of symbiont assortativity, provided further evidence on the importance of bacterial and fungal symbionts in structuring the global plant microbiota network.

[Combined metagenomics and metabolomic analysis of microbial community structure and metabolic function in continuous soybean cropping soils of Songnen Plain, China](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/379410196_Combined_metagenomics_and_metabolomic_analysis_of_microbial_community_structure_and_metabolic_function_in_continuous_soybean_cropping_soils_of_Songnen_Plain_China)

Article

- [Letian Xu](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Letian-Xu-2229783353)
- [Shun Jin](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Shun-Jin-2277936825)
- [Yue Su](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Yue-Su-2277939217)
- [![Chunmei Ma](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Chunmei Ma](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Chunmei-Ma-3)

Continuous cropping has a negative effect on soybean yield. In this study, a positioning experiment was conducted starting in 2015, with three treatments: maize–soybean rotation (SMR), 2-year maize, 2-year soybean rotation cropping (SC2), and 8-year soybean continuous cropping (SC8). We determined soybean yields (2015–2022) and analyzed soil microbial communities, functions, and metabolites composition in the 0–20 cm tillage layer using metagenomics technology and GC–MS technology during soybean flowering in 2022. Results indicated that continuous cropping (SC8) significantly reduced soybean yield compared to crop rotation (SMR) during the experimental period, while SC8 showed higher yield than SC2 in 2022. Compared to SMR, SC8 significantly increased soil N content and significantly decreased pH and TP, AP, and AK content. However, the pH and AK contents of SC8 were significantly higher than those of SC2. LeFSe analysis showed that Friedmanniella, Microlunatus, Nitrososphaera, Rubrobacter, Geodermatophilus, Nitriliruptor were enriched in SC8. Gaiella, Sphaerobacter, Methyloceanibacter were enriched in SC2. Sphingomonas, Cryobacterium, Marmoricola, Haliangium, Arthrobacter, Ramlibacter, Rhizobacter, Pseudolabrys, Methylibium, Variovorax were enriched in SMR. And the relative abundance of Cryobacterium, Marmoricola, Haliangium, Arthrobacter, Ramlibacter, Rhizobacter, Methylibium, Variovorax was significantly positively correlated with yield, while the relative abundance of Gaiella and Sphaerobacter was significantly negatively correlated with yield. SC8 significantly increased the abundance of genes in nitrogen metabolism and significantly decreased the abundance of genes related to phosphorus and potassium metabolism compared with SMR. However, the abundance of genes in potassium metabolism was significantly higher in SC8 than in SC2. Metabolomic analysis showed that compared to SMR, SC8 decreased the abundance of carbohydrates, ketones, and lipid. However, the abundance of carbohydrates, ketones, and lipid was significantly higher in SC8 than in SC2. Mantel test showed that soil pH and AK significantly affected soil microbial community, function, and metabolite composition. Correlation analysis showed significant correlation between soil metabolites and microorganisms, metabolic functions.

- [![Jiayi Jing](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Jiayi Jing](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jiayi-Jing-2)
- [![Paolina Garbeva](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Paolina Garbeva](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Paolina-Garbeva)
- [Jos M. Raaijmakers](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Jos-M-Raaijmakers-17985310)
- [![Marnix H Medema](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Marnix H Medema](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Marnix-Medema)

Natural ecosystems harbor a huge reservoir of taxonomically diverse microbes that are important for plant growth and health. The vast diversity of soil microorganisms and their complex interactions make it challenging to pinpoint the main players important for the life support functions microbes can provide to plants, including enhanced tolerance to (a)biotic stress factors. Designing simplified microbial synthetic communities (SynComs) helps reduce this complexity to unravel the molecular and chemical basis and interplay of specific microbiome functions. While SynComs have been successfully employed to dissect microbial interactions or reproduce microbiome-associated phenotypes, the assembly and reconstitution of these communities have often been based on generic abundance patterns or taxonomic identities and co-occurrences but have only rarely been informed by functional traits. Here, we review recent studies on designing functional SynComs to reveal common principles and discuss multidimensional approaches for community design. We propose a strategy for tailoring the design of functional SynComs based on integration of high-throughput experimental assays with microbial strains and computational genomic analyses of their functional capabilities.

[View](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/379366841_Strategies_for_tailoring_functional_microbial_synthetic_communities)

Show abstract

[Going to extremes: progress in exploring new environments for novel antibiotics](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/379278242_Going_to_extremes_progress_in_exploring_new_environments_for_novel_antibiotics)

Article

Full-text available

- Mar 2024

- [![Gerry/Gerre A Quinn](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Gerry/Gerre A Quinn](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Gerry-gerre-Quinn)
- [![Paul Dyson](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Paul Dyson](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Paul-Dyson-3)

The discoveries of penicillin and streptomycin were pivotal for infection control with the knowledge subsequently being used to enable the discovery of many other antibiotics currently used in clinical practice. These valuable compounds are generally derived from mesophilic soil microorganisms, predominantly Streptomyces species. Unfortunately, problems with the replication of results suggested that this discovery strategy was no longer viable, motivating a switch to combinatorial chemistry in conjunction with existing screening programmes to derive new antimicrobials. However, the chemical space occupied by these synthetic products is vastly reduced compared to those of natural products. More recent approaches such as using artificial intelligence to ‘design’ synthetic ligands to dock with molecular targets suggest that chemical synthesis is still a promising option for discovery. It is important to employ diverse discovery strategies to combat the worrying increase in antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Here, we reconsider whether nature can supply innovative solutions to recalcitrant infections. Specifically, we assess progress in identifying novel antibiotic-producing organisms from extreme and unusual environments. Many of these organisms have adapted physiologies which often means they produce different repertoires of bioactive metabolites compared to their mesophilic counterparts, including antibiotics. In addition, we examine insights into the regulation of extremotolerant bacterial physiologies that can be harnessed to increase the production of clinically important antibiotics and stimulate the synthesis of new antibiotics in mesophilic microorganisms. Finally, we comment on the insights provided by combinatorial approaches to the treatment of infectious diseases that might enhance the efficacy of antibiotics and reduce the development of AMR.

[View](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/379278242_Going_to_extremes_progress_in_exploring_new_environments_for_novel_antibiotics)

[Culturomics-and metagenomics-based insights into the microbial community and function of rhizosphere soils in Sinai desert farming systems](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/384108651_Culturomics-and_metagenomics-based_insights_into_the_microbial_community_and_function_of_rhizosphere_soils_in_Sinai_desert_farming_systems)

Article

- Jan 2023

- [![Wenhui Lian](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Wenhui Lian](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Wenhui-Lian-2)
- [![Osama A. A. Mohamad](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Osama A. A. Mohamad](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Osama-A-A-Mohamad)
- [![Lei Dong 董雷](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Lei Dong 董雷](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Lei-Dong-donglei)
- [![Wen-Jun Li](<Base64-Image-Removed>)Wen-Jun Li](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Wen-Jun-Li)

Background:
The microbiome of the Sinai Desert farming system plays an important role in the adaptive strategy of growing crops in a harsh, poly-extreme, desert environment. However, the diversity and function of microbial communities under this unfavorable moisture and nutritional conditions have not yet been investigated. Based on culturomic and metagenomic methods, we analyzed the microbial diversity and function of a total of fourteen rhizosphere soil samples (collected from twelve plants in four farms of the Sinai desert), which may provide a valuable and meaningful guidance for the design of microbial inoculants.

Results:
The results revealed a wide range of microbial taxa, including a high proportion of novel undescribed lineages. The composition of the rhizosphere microbial communities differed according to the sampling sites, despite similarities or differences in floristics. Whereas, the functional features of rhizosphere microbiomes were significantly similar in different sampling sites, although the microbial communities and the plant hosts themselves were different. Importantly, microorganisms involved in ecosystem functions are different between the sampling sites, for example nitrogen fixation was prevalent in all sample sites while microorganisms responsible for this process were different.

Conclusion:
Here, we provide the first characterization of microbial communities and functions of rhizosphere soil from the Sinai desert farming systems and highlight its unexpectedly high diversity. This study provides evidence that the key microorganisms involved in ecosystem functions are different between sampling sites with different environment conditions, emphasizing the importance of the functional microbiomes of rhizosphere microbial communities. Furthermore, we suggest that microbial inoculants to be used in future agricultural production should select microorganisms that can be involved in plant-microorganism interactions and are already adapted to a similar environmental setting.

[Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Healthcare](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/380124757_Artificial_Intelligence_and_Machine_Learning_in_Healthcare)

Chapter

- Apr 2024

- [Nilanjana Sarkar](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Nilanjana-Sarkar-2273633945)
- [Sumit Goel](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Sumit-Goel-2143112555)

Artificial intelligence (AI) systems are systems designed by humans that, given a complex goal, act in the physical or digital dimension by perceiving their environment through data acquisition, interpreting the collected structured or unstructured data, reasoning on the knowledge, or processing the information, derived from this data and deciding the best action(s) to take to achieve the given goal. It is precisely AI's ability to carry out speedy processing and analysis of datasets that is one of its key strengths. The recent renaissance in AI largely has been driven by the successful application of deep learning — which involves training an artificial neural network with many layers (that is, a ‘deep' neural network) on huge datasets. The rise and dissemination of AI in clinical medicine will refine our diagnostic accuracy and rule-out capabilities. In this Book Chapter, we focus on the AI applications that could augment or change clinical practice, identify the impact arising from the development of AI diagnostic systems and suggest future research directions.

Advancements in genome mining, high-throughput sequencing and experimental techniques have generated an enormous amount of data on natural products. This has led to the design and development of advanced machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms which have simplified the search for novel natural products in the 21st century. These algorithms could effectively analyse the chemical structure of natural products and predict their biological function. They could also effectively analyse large sets of data in a sophisticated manner. In this context, this article reviews the various AI/ML algorithms employed in natural products-based drug discovery. Particular attention is paid to case studies employing AI tools in plant and microbial research. Challenges associated with the use of AI tools for natural products research have also been discussed.

# From POC to Patient: Validating AI-Orchestrated Bioinformatics with a Complete Cancer Case

[![Lynn Langit](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*JCyGL1nZ5hq6mjInmsP_mQ.png)](https://lynnlangit.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--19b6af8fccf2---------------------------------------)

[Lynn Langit](https://lynnlangit.medium.com/?source=post_page---byline--19b6af8fccf2---------------------------------------)

Follow

6 min read

·

Dec 10, 2025

3

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D19b6af8fccf2&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Flynnlangit.medium.com%2Ffrom-poc-to-patient-validating-ai-orchestrated-bioinformatics-with-a-complete-cancer-case-19b6af8fccf2&source=---header_actions--19b6af8fccf2---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

Part 2 of my MCP bioinformatics journey — this time with a synthetic patient, multi-modal data, and a path to production

A few weeks ago, I wrote about building [eight MCP servers for spatial transcriptomics](https://medium.com/@lynnlangit/when-ai-meets-spatial-genomics-8899f883f3b2?sk=2ca68d923e878f64b84e9e8cc62c7722). The response surprised me. Several researchers reached out asking the same question:

> _“Does this actually work on a real analysis?”_

Fair point. Running FASTQ validation is one thing. Orchestrating a complete multi-modal cancer analysis — clinical data, genomics, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, imaging — that’s something else entirely.

So I built patient-one.

## Meet Sarah Anderson

Sarah Elizabeth Anderson doesn’t exist. She’s a synthetic patient I created to stress-test the architecture. But her case is biologically plausible, clinically realistic, and complicated enough to actually matter.

Unfortunately I have actual domain expertise here, as I was recently the lead patient advocate for a dear friend, not unlike Sarah, who was diagnosed with stage IV HGSOC Nov 2025. After many courses of standard treatment, she unfortunately, passed away August 2026.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*HWzBqX908rdIjWVJvZuDbQ.png)

Patient-one Data

The clinical question driving everything: _How can we integrate multi-modal data to understand her resistance mechanisms and identify therapeutic options?_

This isn’t a toy problem. Platinum resistance in ovarian cancer is one of the hardest challenges in gynecologic oncology. The answer requires synthesizing data across modalities that don’t usually talk to each other.

## Expanding the Server Ecosystem

The original eight servers weren’t enough. For a complete patient analysis, I needed two more:

**mcp-multiomics** — Integrates RNA, protein, and phosphorylation data. Runs HAllA (Hierarchical All-against-All) for cross-omics associations and Stouffer’s meta-analysis to combine p-values across modalities.

**mcp-pubmed** — Literature search and full-text retrieval. Because every finding should connect to published evidence.

That brings us to ten specialized servers:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*3QwcoWlUR7XxKJHn1xTJ-A.png)

MCP Servers created for this scenario

Each still does one thing well. But now they can be orchestrated into something more than the sum of their parts.

## The Five-Test Methodology

Here’s what I learned building patient-one: you can’t validate a complex system all at once. You need progressive complexity — each layer building on the previous, each test confirming assumptions before adding more.

**Test 1: Clinical Foundation**

Start with what you know. Query patient demographics, family history, BRCA1 status. Pull lab results and analyze the CA-125 tumor marker trajectory.

Sarah’s CA-125 tells the story: 1456 U/mL at diagnosis → 22 U/mL after platinum therapy (great response!) → 389 U/mL six months later (resistance). This is the classic platinum-resistant pattern that oncologists dread.

Parse the somatic variants: TP53 R175H (hotspot mutation), PIK3CA E545K (activating), PTEN loss of heterozygosity. Compare to TCGA-OV and confirm molecular subtype C1 (immunoreactive).

_Servers used: mockepic, fgbio, tcga_

**Test 2: Multi-Omics Integration**

Now layer in the omics data. RNA expression, proteomics, phospho-proteomics — three views of the same biology.

Run HAllA to find associations across modalities. Apply Stouffer’s method to combine statistical significance. The PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway lights up across all three — consistent with the PIK3CA mutation we found in Test 1.

_Servers used: multiomics, tcga_

**Test 3: Spatial Context**

Expression data tells you _what_. Spatial data tells you _where_.

Process the 10x Visium data with quality filtering. Calculate Moran’s I for spatial autocorrelation — are the marker genes clustering spatially or distributed randomly?

EPCAM (epithelial marker): Moran’s I = 0.72. Strong spatial clustering. The tumor cells are spatially organized, not scattered.

CD3D (immune marker): Moran’s I = 0.45. Moderate clustering. Immune infiltration is present but not uniformly distributed.

This spatial heterogeneity matters for treatment planning. Some tumor regions are immune-hot, others are immune-cold.

_Servers used: spatialtools, fgbio_

**Test 4: Imaging Validation**

The molecular data says there’s heterogeneity. Can we see it?

Fetch the H&E and immunofluorescence images. Run DeepCell for deep learning cell segmentation. Classify phenotypes: epithelial, stromal, immune.

Remember me for faster sign in

The segmentation confirms what the spatial transcriptomics suggested — distinct tumor compartments with variable immune infiltration. The imaging provides ground truth for the molecular predictions.

_Servers used: openimagedata, deepcell_

**Test 5: Synthesis**

This is where it gets interesting. No new data — just integration.

Rank resistance mechanisms by evidence strength across modalities. Generate treatment recommendations with evidence levels. Create a monitoring strategy based on the specific markers that tracked with progression.

The output isn’t just a data dump. It’s a clinically actionable synthesis.

_Servers used: All of the above, orchestrated_

## What the Architecture Actually Produces

Here’s the thing about bioinformatics: the analysis is only useful if someone can act on it.

Patient-one generates outputs for three audiences:

**For Developers:** Complete audit trail. Every MCP server call, every parameter, every intermediate result. Reproducibility is non-negotiable.

**For Care Teams:** Clinical summary with ranked findings, treatment options with evidence levels, suggested monitoring markers. Written in clinical language, not bioinformatics jargon.

**For Patients:** Plain-language explanation of what the tests found and what it means. No jargon. No p-values. Just “here’s what we learned about your cancer and what we can do about it.”

Press enter or click to view image in full size![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:2522/1*2yKJj9KrR-vlQ5IcwY3mLg.png)

Press enter or click to view image in full size![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:2514/1*pefFGYXuPeprgR0dptc18A.png)

Partial output for patient information

Same underlying analysis. Different presentations for different needs. Claude handles the translation.

## The Path to Production

Shown below is a simplified architecture on GCP.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*5zMsR2SFbq2pb_eutWxKjA.png)

Integrating MCP Servers with GCP A2A Protocol

Patient-one is validation, not deployment. For this to work in a real hospital, several things need to happen:

**HIPAA Compliance:** mockepic is a simulation. Real EHR integration requires FHIR-compliant connectors, proper authentication, audit logging that meets regulatory requirements.

**Infrastructure:** The servers need to run somewhere. I’m working on a GCP deployment pattern using Cloud Run for the MCP servers and Vertex AI for the orchestration layer.

**Agent-to-Agent Communication:** Google’s A2A framework provides enterprise-scale agent orchestration. The MCP servers become tools that A2A agents can coordinate. This separates the bioinformatics logic (MCP) from the enterprise orchestration (A2A).

**Validation:** Patient-one is synthetic. Before clinical deployment, we need validation against real cohorts with known outcomes. Does the system’s subtyping match pathologist assessment? Do the predicted resistance mechanisms correlate with actual treatment response?

I’m currently scoping this with a collaborator at a major US research hospital. After we validate it, we’ll have a template for conversational bioinformatics in clinical settings.

## What I Got Wrong the First Time

Building patient-one taught me things that eight servers in isolation couldn’t:

**Integration is harder than analysis.** Each individual server works fine. Making them work _together_ coherently — that’s where the complexity lives. The orchestration prompts matter as much as the tool implementations.

**Synthetic data is an art.** Patient-one’s data had to be biologically plausible, clinically realistic, and internally consistent across modalities. The CA-125 trajectory had to match the treatment timeline. The spatial patterns had to align with the imaging. Creating good synthetic data took longer than building some of the servers.

**Output format is not an afterthought.** I originally focused entirely on the analysis. But a 50-page technical report doesn’t help a patient understand their situation. Building the multi-audience output generation was a significant extension to the original architecture.

## Try It

The patient-one code is in the [spatial-mcp repository](https://github.com/lynnlangit/spatial-mcp) under `architecture/patient-one`.

You’ll find:

- Synthetic data files (clinical, genomic, multi-omics, spatial, imaging)
- Test prompts for all five workflow stages
- Expected outputs for validation
- Documentation for the two new servers (multiomics, pubmed)

If you’re working on something similar — multi-modal integration, clinical AI, conversational bioinformatics — I’d like to hear about it. The field moves faster when we share what works (and what doesn’t).

_Lynn Langit is an independent cloud architect specializing in genomic pipelines and bioinformatics platforms. She’s an AWS Community Hero, Google Cloud Developer Expert, and Microsoft Regional Director currently exploring AI orchestration for precision oncology._

## Abstract

PromptBio is a modular AI platform for scalable, reproducible, and user-adaptable bioinformatics analysis, powered by generative AI and natural language interaction. It supports three complementary modes of analysis designed to meet diverse research needs. PromptGenie is a multi-agent system that enables stepwise, human-in-the-loop workflows using prevalidated domain-standard tools. Within PromptGenie, specialized agents—including DataAgent, OmicsAgent, AnalysisAgent, and QAgent—collaborate to manage tasks such as data ingestion, pipeline execution, statistical analysis, and interactive summarization. DiscoverFlow provides integrated, automated workflows for large-scale multi-omics analysis, offering end-to-end execution and streamlined orchestration. ToolsGenie complements these modes by dynamically generating executable bioinformatics code for custom, user-defined analyses, enabling flexibility beyond standardized workflows. PromptGenie and DiscoverFlow leverage a suite of domain-specific tools, including Omics Tools for standardized omics pipelines, Analysis Tools for downstream statistical interpretation, and MLGenie for machine learning and multi-omics modeling. We present the design, capabilities, and validation of these components, highlight their integration into automated and customizable workflows, and discuss extensibility, monitoring, and compliance. PromptBio aims to democratize high-throughput bioinformatics through a large language model–powered, natural language understanding, workflow generation and agent orchestration.

## 1 Introduction

Bioinformatics data analysis requires processing high-dimensional, heterogeneous datasets, such as genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and biomedical literature. Traditional pipelines often lack flexibility and scalability, prompting the development of AI-driven solutions. The PromptBio platform addresses this need through a modular, multi-agent system powered by generative AI, enabling automated, interpretable, and AI-human collaborative bioinformatics analysis. This paper presents the design and implementation of PromptBio, which coordinates specialized AI agents across data management, analysis, prediction, summarization, and interpretation tasks.

The advent of large language models (LLMs) is transforming bioinformatics by enabling natural language–driven workflows that reduce technical barriers and simplify complex analyses \[ [1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-1)\]. Incumbent platforms like DNAnexus \[ [2](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-2)\] and Seqera \[ [3](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-3)\] have advanced bioinformatics infrastructure but remain dependent on manual configuration and domain-specific expertise. In contrast, emerging AI-first platforms—such as Biomni \[ [4](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-4)\], FutureHouse \[ [5](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-5)\], Kepler AI \[ [6](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-6)\], and Potato AI \[ [7](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-7)\]— offer flexible, intelligent systems that broaden access to data-driven discovery. PromptBio ( [https://promptbio.ai](https://promptbio.ai/)) contributes to this evolution by offering an industrial-grade, agent-based platform that delivers value through: (1) making data analysis intuitive and engaging, (2) empowering users across technical skill levels, and (3) providing comprehensive, cost-effective solutions ( [https://promptbio.ai/use-cases.html](https://promptbio.ai/use-cases.html)).

Designing next-generation bioinformatics platforms demands adherence to several key principles. First, conversational interfaces driven by LLMs promote accessibility and ease of use \[ [8](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-8)\]. Second, modular, multi-agent design enables scalable, parallelized workflows for tasks like multi-omics integration \[ [1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-1)\]. Third, robust evaluation protocols ensure scientific validity and reproducibility \[ [9](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-9)\]. Fourth, real-time monitoring supports fault detection and system optimization \[ [10](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-10)\]. Fifth, cloud-native infrastructure supports scalability and cost-efficiency \[ [11](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-11)\], while strong security and regulatory compliance (e.g., HIPAA) ensure data protection \[ [12](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#ref-12)\]. Finally, extensibility allows the platform to adapt to emerging omics technologies and evolving analysis needs.

PromptBio distinguishes between three complementary modes of analysis:

- (1) PromptGenie – a multi-agent system for managing and processing omics data, machine learning tasks, and tabular data operations. It leverages domain-standard, prevalidated tools with embedded best practices and emphasizes human-in-the-loop design to ensure precision and reproducibility.

- (2) DiscoverFlow – an orchestration utility that streamlines complex workflows and supports end-to-end execution. DiscoverFlow enables automated, large-scale multi-omics analyses, facilitating the integration and management of diverse analytical components within a unified framework.

- (3) ToolsGenie – a generative engine that empowers users to design and execute custom analyses tailored to specific research questions. ToolsGenie provides the flexibility needed for exploratory and innovative workflows, complementing standardized approaches with user-driven adaptability.


The platform’s intuitive, conversation-driven interface allows users—both expert and novice—to execute sophisticated bioinformatics tasks such as data preprocessing, clustering, differential analysis, and biomarker discovery without writing code. PromptBio’s multi-agent structure functions as a virtual interdisciplinary team, integrating expertise across data science, biology, and clinical research into a cohesive analytical unit. This design reduces communication overhead, accelerates analysis, and enables individual users to achieve team-level productivity.

In this paper, we describe the platform’s design, core tools, workflow orchestration, and operational safeguards. [Section 2](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-2) introduces the multi-agent design. [Section 3](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-11) presents the suite of built-in tools, including Omics Tools, Analyses Tools, MLGenie, and MarkerGenie. [Section 4](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-18) describes DiscoverFlow, a framework for orchestrating automated multi-omics workflows. [Section 5](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-22) discusses ToolsGenie, which supports on-demand, user-defined analyses via generative AI. [Section 6](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-26) details platform monitoring, compliance, and security, and [Section 7](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-30) outlines future directions toward fully agentic AI systems.

## 2 Multi-Agent Design

PromptBio’s multi-agent system ( [Figure 1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#F1)) consists of a supervisor agent, PromptGenie, which coordinates a list of specialized agents—including the **OpsAgent, DataAgent, OmicsAgent, AnalysisAgent, and QAgent**—that collaboratively manage the full lifecycle of omics and machine learning tasks. These agents rely on prevalidated tools and domain-standard practices to ensure analysis accuracy, reproducibility, and interpretability.

Design of the PromptBio multi-agent system, depicting interactions between PromptGenie and specialized agents, including OpsAgent, DataAgent, OmicsAgent, AnalysisAgent, and QAgent, and integrated tools.

Built on a modular and extensible design, PromptGenie enables the integration of new tools and supports task decomposition for scalable, parallel execution. Importantly, it incorporates a human-in-the-loop design, enabling domain experts to review intermediate steps, guide decision points, and validate outputs.

### 2.1 PromptGenie

PromptGenie, the supervisor agent, defines standard communication protocols, unified data schemas, and a shared runtime environment to ensure seamless operation across specialized agents.

At its core is an Agent Registry—a centralized directory that maps analytical tasks to appropriate agents (e.g., DataAgent, OmicsAgent) based on their declared capabilities. This enables targeted and efficient task routing.

User queries are submitted in natural language and interpreted by integrated LLMs, which translate them into executable steps. These steps are then routed to agents for immediate execution in a human-in-the-loop process that enables guided, interactive refinement. For example, commands such as “ _Apply RNA-seq analysi_ s” and “ _Find differentially expressed genes_” are processed step by step, ensuring transparency, control, and reproducibility.

By integrating agentic execution with natural language interfaces, PromptGenie streamlines bioinformatics workflows in a way that is accessible to novices and powerful enough for experts—ensuring scalability, reproducibility, and ease of use within a structured yet adaptable framework.

### 2.2 OpsAgent

OpsAgent serves as the controller for data management, enforcing strict structural and relational integrity across all core entities—samples, subjects, datasets. It exposes a transactional interface for CRUD (create, read, update, delete) operations with atomicity guarantees and versioned audit trails, ensuring all modifications are consistent and traceable. This coordination abstracts away low-level data logistics, reducing operational overhead and minimizing the risk of duplication or corruption in collaborative environments—providing a robust foundation for scalable, compliant bioinformatics workflows.

In addition to data-layer control, OpsAgent adopts a namespaced project model that enables secure operations and collaborations. Projects function as isolated workspaces where users, tasks, and data objects are logically grouped, with OpsAgent coordinating membership, task assignment, and data bindings. This coordination layer enforces consistent interface contracts and guarantees that downstream analytical agents operate on schema-conformant, semantically validated inputs. It also supports fine-grained permission settings through role-based access control (RBAC), allowing users to be assigned scoped privileges—such as read, write, or administrative access—ensuring that collaborative activities occur within clearly defined security boundaries. By coupling strict input governance with robust access control, OpsAgent facilitates secure, reproducible, and scalable execution across multi-agent pipelines while supporting seamless collaboration among users.

### 2.3 DataAgent

DataAgent streamlines data discovery and acquisition for bioinformatics analyses by addressing two key user needs:

1. Effective retrieval of relevant data from public datasets through natural language query.

2. Seamless access to retrieved data via efficient cloud-to-cloud transfer.


The goal is to comprehensively and accurately identify the most relevant publicly available datasets based on user queries, while also assessing whether the data is directly downloadable. For datasets with controlled access, the system allows users to provide access credentials or tokens, enabling secure and straightforward data import.

DataAgent integrates a broad range of specialized repositories that host, manage, and annotate diverse types of biological and genomic data. These curated sources are critical to advancing scientific research by providing structured and reliable access to large-scale datasets. A full list of supported databases is provided in [Supplementary Table 1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#T1), covering the majority of commonly used omics data types and repositories. This list is readily extensible to incorporate additional data sources as needed.

#### 2.3.1 Query Processing and Semantic Search

DataAgent streamlines the dataset search process by combining natural language understanding with structured search capabilities. Large Language Models (LLMs) parse user queries (e.g., “ _I would like to obtain RNA-seq data for Alzheimer’s disease with a sample size greater than 30_”) and extract both the core search intent (e.g., “ _RNA-seq data for Alzheimer’s disease_”) and specific filtering criteria (e.g., “ _sample size greater than 30_”). A hybrid search engine then integrates semantic similarity—matching user intent to dataset descriptions in metadata—with keyword-based retrieval and metadata-aware filtering to efficiently surface the most relevant datasets.

To assess DataAgent’s retrieval accuracy, we conducted a benchmark study, detailed in the **[Supplementary Table 2](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#T2)**. The results demonstrate its effectiveness in returning datasets that align closely with both the semantic content and specific conditions of user queries.

#### 2.3.2 Efficient Cloud-to-Cloud Data Transfer

DataAgent enables direct cloud-to-platform data transfers from AWS S3 and other cloud-hosted data repositories, bypassing the burden of transferring large files through local machines. By automating authentication and leveraging secure repository APIs, DataAgent ensures both speed and data security. It is also equipped with fault-tolerant features such as automatic transfer resumption to handle transient network interruptions. All retrieved data and metadata are ingested into the PromptBio data system, making them immediately available for downstream analysis. Designed for scalability, DataAgent supports diverse omics data types—enabling streamlined, secure integration across a broad range of research applications.

### 2.4 OmicsAgent

OmicsAgent delivers standardized, scalable solutions for omics data processing and analytical workflows. The platform supports a comprehensive suite of pipelines for multi-omics data types and commonly used downstream analysis modules. Each solution is optimized for performance and adherence to domain-specific best practices, ensuring reproducibility. For a detailed technical overview of the supported omics pipelines, analytical modules, and implementation details, refer to [Section 3.1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#sec-12) and 3.2.

### 2.5 AnalysisAgent

AnalysisAgent is designed to meet flexible, custom analytical needs for tabular data that fall outside the scope of standard OmicsAgent functionality. It empowers researchers with intelligent, on-demand analytics through two complementary modes of interaction, each optimized for different levels of complexity and transparency.

- **Fast Analysis Mode** is tailored for rapid prototyping and lightweight, results-oriented tasks. Users can submit queries in natural language—such as “ _What is the dimension of my input data matrix?_” or “ _Create a scatter plot of the gene expression values of EGFR vs. PIK3CA_”—and receive responses from automatically generated Python or R scripts execution results. The outputs include summary, visualizations, and concise interpretations that provide immediate insight without requiring manual coding.

- **Notebook Mode** supports more complex, exploratory workflows where transparency and reproducibility are critical. In response to detailed prompts, AnalysisAgent generates well-structured Jupyter notebooks with clean, annotated code, embedded results, and interpretations. For example, a user might request: “ _Merge data1.csv and data2.csv, apply batch effect correction, compare the PCA results before and after correction._” These notebooks are fully executable within the PromptBio environment, enabling users to inspect, modify, rerun, and share analytical workflows with ease.


By uniting intelligent code synthesis, contextual understanding, and automatic execution, AnalysisAgent ensures that researchers can efficiently obtain actionable insights tailored to their specific scientific questions.

### 2.5 QAgent

QAgent is designed to streamline user interactions and enhance the bioinformatics analysis experience. By combining Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with large language models (LLMs), QAgent delivers accurate, context-aware responses while maintaining strict data privacy where each user is restricted for queries under his own workspace.

QAgent addresses three main types of questions using distinct information sources ( [Figure 2](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#F2)):

- **General knowledge questions** are answered using the LLM’s own knowledge base (e.g., “ _What is the TP53 gene?_”).

- **Platform-related questions** are resolved by referencing curated documentation from a vector database, providing detailed guidance on functionalities, tool options, and usage instructions (e.g., “ _What is the PromptBio genomics pipeline?_”). An example is shown in **[Supplementary Figure 1](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#F5)**.

- **User workspace questions** are addressed by accessing relevant workspace information, such as project or task identifiers, to extract findings or results (e.g., “ _What are the key findings of the differential analysis for task ID …?_”). An example is shown in **[Supplementary Figure 2](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.07.05.663295v1.full#F6)**.
